textPlain: Keep in mind that Kimmel has been hinting about retiring for a couple years now, his contract was up in the air, the "late night television show" category is evaporating (if there's still even a Tonight Show in 10 years, it'll be purely for nostalgia), and this sends Kimmel out in a blaze of glory.I think it's too easy to sort of anthropomorphize these kinds of conflicts --- Kimmel's show has a large staff, and he's responsible for their livelihoods --- but it wouldn't be totally out of the question that Kimmel steered right into this.There's nothing new about this, though: ABC also took Bill Maher off the air, 20 years ago, almost identical circumstances. Maher wound up at HBO. Kimmel will wind up on a podcast, and, like Conan, probably gain in relevance.
The right, has for the past decade or so taken a moral high ground with regards to cancelation. Seems that now they've adopted a "turnaround is fair play" mentality.
I thought we had freedom of speech, and we're broadly against "cancel culture".

Sounds like it's time for ole' Jimmy to just give up and retire... there's no point in fighting this anymore and I'm sure his family is ready for him to quit anyway.We've sure come a long way from The Man Show.
Believe it or not, alienating politics isn't great for business.  Neither is peddling conspiracies.
Hey look it’s cancel culture all over again
The right is simply good at PR. People forget that they invented cancel culture. Dixie Chicks anyone?

It's only cancel culture when the left does it.And this is infinitely worse than a bunch of internet commenters disagreeing with his speech. Trump and the FCC directly threatened to pull ABC's license unless they regulated his speech, and ABC caved.
